The Lighthouse Keeper's Journal

On the first night the wind was kind and the lamp burned steady. I climbed the spiral stair with my logbook tucked under one arm and a tin mug in the other. The sea, far below, breathed the slow breath of a sleeping animal. I counted three ships on the horizon, their lights small and patient.

The second night brought rain and a stranger noise from the rocks. I thought at first it was the gulls, but gulls do not speak in syllables. I leaned over the rail with my lantern raised and saw nothing, only the white spray rising and falling. The lamp turned its great eye through the storm without complaint.

By the third night I had stopped sleeping in the keeper's bed and taken to dozing on the stair. The lamp needs an attendant who can rise quickly, and dreams down here come slow and heavy. I keep my logbook open on the rail, the ink already smudged by the salt that finds its way through every crack in the glass.

On the fourth night the bell on the buoy rang seven times when it had no business ringing at all. The water was flat as a plate. I wrote the time in the margin and underlined it twice, then walked the whole gallery with my lantern. A small wet bootprint waited at the door, pointing toward the stair. I am not afraid, only attentive.

The fifth night was quiet again, and the supply boat came at dawn with letters and a sack of coal. The young man in the bow looked up at me as if he expected to find someone else. I waved and he waved, and afterward I sat with the letters unopened on my knee for almost an hour.

Tonight is the sixth night, and the lamp is steady, and the sea is steady, and the logbook waits with its dry page. I will write something true here before I climb back up. Whatever walks below the gallery may keep walking. The light is mine to tend, and tend it I will, until the relief boat comes on Sunday or the wind takes the tower whole.
